persons.arguments_against: There are 8 Kirkwoods in the staff lists and 3 bios with this surname, so a same-name relative could in principle hold a Fiji audit post; and the deterministic pass flagged place_inherited (the audit-dept place is inherited, not printed). But this fails as a counter-hypothesis: the match is not on surname or a single generic title, it is on a highly specific multi-step career trajectory unique to one person. The bio reads 'ch. clk. audit dept., 1892-1907; asst. auditor, 1907' and the staff list shows R. H. Kirkwood as Chief Clerk in the Audit Department through 1906 then becoming Assistant Auditor in exactly 1907; the bio's 'sub-collr., customs ... May, 1910' matches the staff list's anomalous 'Sub-Collector of Customs' entry in 1911. Two full forename initials (R. H.) agree, age is consistent (35 in 1894), and 3 editions co-occur. A relative would not replicate this exact promotion sequence and the one-off customs posting.

evidence_cited.fact: sub-collr., customs, and postmr., Lautoka, May, 1910

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second R. H. Kirkwood in the Fiji audit/customs service in the same years, or a printed entry showing the staff-list Chief Clerk/Assistant Auditor was a different person.

notes: Single bio, single stint. Career trajectory (Chief Clerk audit dept -> Assistant Auditor 1907 -> Sub-Collector of Customs ~1910/11) matches verbatim entry precisely; forename initials and age also agree.
